issues: All Tier 1 experiments use 10 epochs, 100-sample subsets, and a single random seed. Two of four blocks (ResNet-18 CIFAR-10: 10.01-10.97% accuracy, ViT-S/4 CIFAR-100: 2.64-2.89%) are at or within 1 pp of the random-chance floor (10% and ~1% respectively). Accuracy comparisons in these degenerate blocks cannot be attributed to augmentation ordering effects and must not be counted toward H1 confirmation. After excluding these blocks, only 2 of the remaining 2 interpretable blocks show spread > 0.5%, not '3/4' as claimed.

The paper presents ordering results (10 epochs, 100 samples) and baseline results (30 epochs, full dataset) in the same table (Table 1), making ordering-vs-baseline comparison invalid. The ordering experiments show 10-46% accuracy while the baselines show 57-92%, but this difference is entirely due to training conditions, not the methods. A reviewer will immediately flag this as methodologically unsound.

The proof sketch of the Ordering-Dependent Generalization Bound (Theorem 1) is missing the core mathematical step. The statement 'By the Lipschitz property, W_2(mu_sigma, mu_sigma') <= sum NC_2(t_i, t_j; mu)' requires a bubble-sort decomposition argument: decomposing sigma -> sigma' into a sequence of adjacent transpositions and applying the triangle inequality at each step. Without this derivation, the bound is unverified. A NeurIPS reviewer with optimal transport background will immediately request this.

The DPI Reversibility Principle has a logical gap: the contraction coefficient eta_i is defined as a worst-case quantity (supremum over all distributions p, q), making it a property of the channel alone, independent of position in the pipeline. The claim that ordering affects information loss requires that contraction be input-distribution-dependent. The worst-case definition defeats the ordering-dependence argument.

The NC_2 proxy was computed on 100 samples with 100 random projections, while the pre-registered plan specifies 10k samples and 1000 projections. The SWD estimate with n=100 has high variance for 32x32x3 = 3072-dimensional images. The current NC_2 values (Crop-CJ: 0.0515, Crop-Flip: 0.0448, Flip-CJ: 0.0348) may not be reliable estimates, and H3's falsification verdict should be marked 'preliminary pending full-scale recomputation'.

The Tier 2 category-ordering result (9.01 pp spread, interleaved P->G vs all-geometric-first) is based on a single seed, 5k samples, 10 epochs, and a single architecture-dataset combination (ResNet-18/CIFAR-10). This is the paper's most striking empirical number but has the least statistical support. The paper calls this 'the largest signal in our study' without adequately flagging that it is also the most likely to be an early-training artifact.

The Tier 3 magnitude sweep uses only 3 data points (M=5, M=9, M=14) with a single seed each. The M=14 result shows exactly identical accuracy (0.245) for both orderings to 3 decimal places, which is suspicious: either both models happened to achieve precisely the same final accuracy (unlikely with different augmentation pipelines even if they converge to similar distributions), or there is a numerical coincidence or data artifact. This needs investigation before H5 is labeled 'falsified'.

The paper's theoretical framing positions the NC bound and DPI reversibility principle as positive contributions, but both are empirically falsified in the pilot data (NC: rho=-0.20, DPI: mixed signals, overall rho=-0.06). If the full-scale experiments confirm the falsifications, the theoretical section must be reframed as 'we proposed these frameworks, tested them, and showed they fail to predict ordering quality -- here is why.' Without this reframing, the theory and evidence are in tension.

H2 is defined inconsistently across the paper. The proposal defines H2 as 'Architecture-dependent ordering sensitivity (ViTs more sensitive to geometric ordering, CNNs to photometric)'. The method section states 'ViTs and CNNs exhibit different ordering preferences'. The results section header says 'H2: Architecture-Dependent Ordering Sensitivity' but the verdict table labels it 'H2: Reversibility-sorted wins'. The DPI-based prediction (CJ->Flip->Crop outperforms conventional) is a separate testable claim that was silently substituted for the original architecture-differential hypothesis.

The InfoNCE MI estimation in Tier 4b uses 100 samples with encoders trained for only 10 epochs. At this scale, the InfoNCE estimator has high variance and may not reflect true mutual information. The CIFAR-10 rho=+0.54 (p=0.20) and CIFAR-100 rho=-0.66 (p=0.08) are both non-significant (n=6 orderings). Reporting the combined rho=-0.057 obscures the opposite signs, which is the most informative feature of this result.

classified_issues.description: All Tier 1 experiments use 10 epochs, 100-sample subsets, and a single random seed. Two of four blocks (ResNet-18 CIFAR-10: 10.01-10.97% accuracy, ViT-S/4 CIFAR-100: 2.64-2.89%) are at or within 1 pp of the random-chance floor (10% and ~1% respectively). Accuracy comparisons in these degenerate blocks cannot be attributed to augmentation ordering effects and must not be counted toward H1 confirmation. After excluding these blocks, only 2 of the remaining 2 interpretable blocks show spread > 0.5%, not '3/4' as claimed.
classified_issues.suggestion: Run full Tier 1: 6 orderings x 2 architectures x 2 datasets x 5 seeds x 200 epochs. Only then can paired t-tests, Bonferroni correction, Cohen's d, and two-way ANOVA be applied as pre-registered. This is the single blocking action before any hypothesis verdict can be stated.

classified_issues.description: The paper presents ordering results (10 epochs, 100 samples) and baseline results (30 epochs, full dataset) in the same table (Table 1), making ordering-vs-baseline comparison invalid. The ordering experiments show 10-46% accuracy while the baselines show 57-92%, but this difference is entirely due to training conditions, not the methods. A reviewer will immediately flag this as methodologically unsound.
classified_issues.suggestion: Remove baselines from Table 1 entirely, or run baselines at the same pilot conditions as the orderings. The asymmetric comparison actively misleads readers regardless of the caveat text.

classified_issues.description: The proof sketch of the Ordering-Dependent Generalization Bound (Theorem 1) is missing the core mathematical step. The statement 'By the Lipschitz property, W_2(mu_sigma, mu_sigma') <= sum NC_2(t_i, t_j; mu)' requires a bubble-sort decomposition argument: decomposing sigma -> sigma' into a sequence of adjacent transpositions and applying the triangle inequality at each step. Without this derivation, the bound is unverified. A NeurIPS reviewer with optimal transport background will immediately request this.
classified_issues.suggestion: Add the bubble-sort decomposition argument explicitly, either inline (4-5 equations) or in a formal appendix labeled 'Proof of Theorem 1'. Show the intermediate permutations and bound each step's W_2 distance by the corresponding NC_2 term.

classified_issues.description: The DPI Reversibility Principle has a logical gap: the contraction coefficient eta_i is defined as a worst-case quantity (supremum over all distributions p, q), making it a property of the channel alone, independent of position in the pipeline. The claim that ordering affects information loss requires that contraction be input-distribution-dependent. The worst-case definition defeats the ordering-dependence argument.
classified_issues.suggestion: Redefine eta_i as a distribution-dependent quantity: eta_i(nu) = sup_{p,q: p,q << nu} D_KL(t_i#p || t_i#q) / D_KL(p || q), where nu is the distribution of the input to channel t_i. Then the argument that placing a lossy transform early changes nu for subsequent channels becomes rigorous.

classified_issues.description: The NC_2 proxy was computed on 100 samples with 100 random projections, while the pre-registered plan specifies 10k samples and 1000 projections. The SWD estimate with n=100 has high variance for 32x32x3 = 3072-dimensional images. The current NC_2 values (Crop-CJ: 0.0515, Crop-Flip: 0.0448, Flip-CJ: 0.0348) may not be reliable estimates, and H3's falsification verdict should be marked 'preliminary pending full-scale recomputation'.
classified_issues.suggestion: Recompute Tier 4a NC_2 with the pre-registered 10k samples / 1000 projections. If rho_s remains near -0.20 at full scale, the falsification is robust. Additionally, test feature-space NC_2 (computing SWD in penultimate-layer feature space of a pretrained ResNet-18) as this was identified in the result debate as the most promising theoretical extension.

classified_issues.description: The Tier 2 category-ordering result (9.01 pp spread, interleaved P->G vs all-geometric-first) is based on a single seed, 5k samples, 10 epochs, and a single architecture-dataset combination (ResNet-18/CIFAR-10). This is the paper's most striking empirical number but has the least statistical support. The paper calls this 'the largest signal in our study' without adequately flagging that it is also the most likely to be an early-training artifact.
classified_issues.suggestion: Before committing to full Tier 2 (18 GPU-h), run a Tier 2 confirmation pilot: full CIFAR-10, 50 epochs, 2 seeds, ResNet-18. If the spread shrinks below 2 pp, the 9.01 number is an artifact. If it holds above 2 pp, proceed to full Tier 2 with 5 seeds. Report results of this intermediate check before final submission.

classified_issues.description: The Tier 3 magnitude sweep uses only 3 data points (M=5, M=9, M=14) with a single seed each. The M=14 result shows exactly identical accuracy (0.245) for both orderings to 3 decimal places, which is suspicious: either both models happened to achieve precisely the same final accuracy (unlikely with different augmentation pipelines even if they converge to similar distributions), or there is a numerical coincidence or data artifact. This needs investigation before H5 is labeled 'falsified'.
classified_issues.suggestion: Verify that M=14 training runs are genuinely independent: different augmentation sequences, independent seeds, no shared cached outputs. Report per-epoch training curves at M=14 for both orderings to confirm the model is learning. Also add at least 2 intermediate magnitude levels (M=7, M=11) to characterize the inverted-U shape with more resolution.

classified_issues.description: The paper's theoretical framing positions the NC bound and DPI reversibility principle as positive contributions, but both are empirically falsified in the pilot data (NC: rho=-0.20, DPI: mixed signals, overall rho=-0.06). If the full-scale experiments confirm the falsifications, the theoretical section must be reframed as 'we proposed these frameworks, tested them, and showed they fail to predict ordering quality -- here is why.' Without this reframing, the theory and evidence are in tension.
classified_issues.suggestion: Restructure the introduction and contribution list to lead with the empirical discovery (ordering matters, ViT > CNN sensitivity, interleaved > block). Present the NC and DPI frameworks as 'natural theoretical candidates that we test and find insufficient', and the gap between pixel-space distributional measures and optimization-mediated outcomes as the novel theoretical finding. This framing is stronger and more honest.

classified_issues.description: H2 is defined inconsistently across the paper. The proposal defines H2 as 'Architecture-dependent ordering sensitivity (ViTs more sensitive to geometric ordering, CNNs to photometric)'. The method section states 'ViTs and CNNs exhibit different ordering preferences'. The results section header says 'H2: Architecture-Dependent Ordering Sensitivity' but the verdict table labels it 'H2: Reversibility-sorted wins'. The DPI-based prediction (CJ->Flip->Crop outperforms conventional) is a separate testable claim that was silently substituted for the original architecture-differential hypothesis.
classified_issues.suggestion: Assign distinct labels: H2a for architecture-differential sensitivity (original formulation, tested via two-way ANOVA), H2b for the DPI reversibility-sorted prediction. Report both in the hypothesis verdict table. The original H2a (architecture x ordering interaction) remains the more scientifically interesting test and is what the pre-registered two-way ANOVA was designed to test.

classified_issues.description: The InfoNCE MI estimation in Tier 4b uses 100 samples with encoders trained for only 10 epochs. At this scale, the InfoNCE estimator has high variance and may not reflect true mutual information. The CIFAR-10 rho=+0.54 (p=0.20) and CIFAR-100 rho=-0.66 (p=0.08) are both non-significant (n=6 orderings). Reporting the combined rho=-0.057 obscures the opposite signs, which is the most informative feature of this result.
classified_issues.suggestion: Display the per-dataset rho values prominently rather than collapsing to combined rho. Note explicitly that the opposite signs (CIFAR-10 positive, CIFAR-100 negative) could indicate either genuine task-difficulty modulation of MI's predictive value or estimator noise. Full-scale MI estimation with properly trained encoders and 10k samples is required before this can be distinguished.

issues: RAVEL cross-domain analysis uses Qwen2.5-0.5B probes (d_model=896) as a proxy for Gemma 2 2B (d_model=2304). None of three probes pass the 85% accuracy gate (71.4%/37.8%/36.8%). Probe directions are projected via random QR decomposition from 896 to 2304 dimensions — semantically invalid cross-model transfer. All 18 absorption rate measurements above-3x-random may be detection of probe-mismatch artifacts, not genuine absorption.

Section 6.2 claims 'EDA ordering (late > partial > early) holds at L12-65k (KW p=0.0002)' but phase2a_taxonomy.json shows eda_ordering_holds=false for both L12-16k and L12-65k at threshold 0.3. At L12-65k, partial median EDA (0.652) < early median EDA (0.668) — the full three-way ordering late > partial > early is not supported. This is a factual error in the manuscript.

EDA fails on 4/6 Gemma Scope configurations (AUROC < 0.65: L5-65k 0.617, L12-65k 0.468, L19-16k 0.458, L19-65k 0.562). The system's pre-registered go/no-go gate (>=3/6 configurations pass) was logged as NO_GO (2/6) but the experiment continued without prominently acknowledging this gate failure. EDA also fails on GPT2-L10 (AUROC=0.336, reversed direction). The paper positions EDA as a general screening tool.

ITAC achieves 3.14% mean FN reduction vs 20% pre-registered target (H5 falsified by 6.4x). Of 10 ITAC targets at L12-65k: 4 have n_parent_candidates=0 (D-EDA decomposition found no parent), 5 of 6 evaluable targets show 0% FN reduction, only j_idx=61217 shows 18.9% improvement. ITAC is evaluated only on synthetic activations from decoder columns. The paper bills ITAC as a 'proof-of-concept correction method.' The 'null test' on early-type latents never actually applies ITAC to early-type latents.

Theorem 1 (EDA Lower Bound) proof sketch is informal and circular. The critical claim — that absorption at a partial minimum produces a gradient perturbation of magnitude proportional to delta*||d_c||*sin(theta_{jc}) — is asserted as a given, not derived from the SDL loss stationarity conditions. The formal definition of delta-absorption as a mathematical quantity does not precede the theorem statement (self-reference to 'Section 3' from within Section 3). The quadratic form delta^2 * sin^2(theta_{jc}) / (2 + delta^2) appears without algebraic derivation.

EDA (1 - cos(encoder_row_j, decoder_col_j)) is nearly identical to the existing SAEBench metric 'encoder_decoder_cosine_sim' (Pearson r > 0.999, reported in the paper itself). The Introduction presents EDA as a new metric without disclosing this pre-existing quantity. The novelty claim depends entirely on the formal theorem (currently informal) and the systematic AUROC evaluation.

With n_pos=16 out of 65,536 latents at L12-65k (0.024% positive prevalence), AUROC is statistically unreliable regardless of label quality. The pilot go/no-go gate used AUROC at enriched prevalence (pilot n_pos/n_total ~16/100) which does not predict full-dataset AUROC at 0.024% prevalence. The AUROC collapse (0.853 pilot -> 0.468 full) was a predictable consequence of the evaluation design, not just label noise.

The H6 scaling analysis uses canonical target L0 values (L0_source='gemma_scope_paper_canonical'), not measured values. L0 variation across the 6 SAE configurations is 65-72, with no variation within layer for different widths. Partial rho(width | L0) cannot separate width and L0 effects. The 'H6 falsification' claim via partial correlation is methodologically invalid.

Three figures (Fig 5, 6, 7) are referenced in the paper text but do not exist as generated PDF files. Generation scripts reference data in phase3e_crossdomain_analysis.json and phase2a_taxonomy.json which exist. Paper cannot be submitted in current state.

The taxonomy's 'early absorption dominance' central finding (72-75%) rests on only 2 SAE configurations (L12-16k underpowered n=16, L12-65k n=65). The paper frames this as 'reframing the absorption problem for the field' without adequate empirical basis. At tau=0.2, the early proportion drops to 32% at L12-65k — the dominance finding is highly threshold-dependent.

The ITAC 'null test' on early-type latents does not test what it claims — it measures pre-existing FN rates without applying ITAC. The paper says '0% FN reduction on early-type latents validates subtype selectivity' but ITAC was never applied to early-type latents in the first place.

The intra-RAVEL Spearman rho=0.924 is presented as 'highly coherent' without noting that city-continent vs. city-language (rho=0.886, p=0.019) does NOT survive Bonferroni correction at the 0.0071 threshold. Only 2 of 3 pairwise comparisons are Bonferroni-significant.

SynthSAEBench validation (AUROC=1.000) is tautological — absorption is injected as encoder-decoder misalignment, the exact pattern EDA measures. This validates that EDA detects geometric misalignment, not that EDA detects behavioral absorption as defined by Chanin et al.

Abstract is 247 words vs NeurIPS 200-word target. Several abstract claims lack configuration-specific qualifiers ('+0.396 AUROC' is L5-16k specific; 'all 18 measurements exceed 3x baseline' omits proxy probe caveat; 'reframes the absorption problem' overclaims from n=2 configurations).

The self-referential definition of delta in Theorem 1 ('defined formally in Section 3' while inside Section 3) creates a circular reference. A formal definition of delta-absorption as a mathematical quantity must precede the theorem statement.

classified_issues.description: RAVEL cross-domain analysis uses Qwen2.5-0.5B probes (d_model=896) as a proxy for Gemma 2 2B (d_model=2304). None of three probes pass the 85% accuracy gate (71.4%/37.8%/36.8%). Probe directions are projected via random QR decomposition from 896 to 2304 dimensions — semantically invalid cross-model transfer. All 18 absorption rate measurements above-3x-random may be detection of probe-mismatch artifacts, not genuine absorption.
classified_issues.suggestion: Obtain Gemma 2 2B HuggingFace access and retrain RAVEL probes on the correct model (~3-5 GPU hours). Alternatively, run the entire Phase 3 pipeline on GPT-2 Small where model access is unrestricted. If neither is feasible within next iteration, reframe Section 5 as a feasibility study removing all quantitative absorption rate claims.
classified_issues.verification: All 3 RAVEL probes achieve >=85% accuracy on Gemma 2 2B or chosen accessible model; max_cos_with_probe > tau=0.3 for at least some latents

classified_issues.description: Section 6.2 claims 'EDA ordering (late > partial > early) holds at L12-65k (KW p=0.0002)' but phase2a_taxonomy.json shows eda_ordering_holds=false for both L12-16k and L12-65k at threshold 0.3. At L12-65k, partial median EDA (0.652) < early median EDA (0.668) — the full three-way ordering late > partial > early is not supported. This is a factual error in the manuscript.
classified_issues.suggestion: Correct Section 6.2 to accurately state: 'The ordering late > early holds at all 5 tested thresholds for both configurations (L12-65k KW p=0.0002; L12-16k KW p=0.24, underpowered). However, the predicted full ordering late > partial > early does not hold: partial median EDA is consistently equal to or below early median EDA, indicating the decoder cosine similarity threshold used for early/partial boundary classification does not correspond to EDA rank ordering.' This correction is mandatory before any further writing work.
classified_issues.verification: Section 6.2 text matches the eda_ordering_holds=false field in phase2a_taxonomy.json

classified_issues.description: EDA fails on 4/6 Gemma Scope configurations (AUROC < 0.65: L5-65k 0.617, L12-65k 0.468, L19-16k 0.458, L19-65k 0.562). The system's pre-registered go/no-go gate (>=3/6 configurations pass) was logged as NO_GO (2/6) but the experiment continued without prominently acknowledging this gate failure. EDA also fails on GPT2-L10 (AUROC=0.336, reversed direction). The paper positions EDA as a general screening tool.
classified_issues.suggestion: Reframe EDA as a regime-specific screening tool with explicit operating conditions: 'EDA provides reliable absorption screening at mid-layer, narrow-dictionary SAEs (empirically validated at L5-16k and L12-16k); performance degrades to chance or below at wider dictionaries (65k) and deeper layers.' Add an operating regime table to the paper. Remove universal screening claims from the abstract and Introduction. Explicitly acknowledge the go/no-go gate outcome (NO_GO, 2/6) in Section 4.2.
classified_issues.verification: Abstract and Introduction contain explicit operating regime qualifiers; Section 4.2 acknowledges NO_GO gate result

classified_issues.description: ITAC achieves 3.14% mean FN reduction vs 20% pre-registered target (H5 falsified by 6.4x). Of 10 ITAC targets at L12-65k: 4 have n_parent_candidates=0 (D-EDA decomposition found no parent), 5 of 6 evaluable targets show 0% FN reduction, only j_idx=61217 shows 18.9% improvement. ITAC is evaluated only on synthetic activations from decoder columns. The paper bills ITAC as a 'proof-of-concept correction method.' The 'null test' on early-type latents never actually applies ITAC to early-type latents.
classified_issues.suggestion: Present ITAC as a negative result with a mechanistic explanation: 'ITAC reveals that late-type encoder suppression is more severe than linear projection recovery can address; only 1 of 10 targeted latents shows meaningful FN improvement (18.9%), and 4 of 10 have no identifiable parent candidates.' Remove ITAC from the abstract as a correction method. Demote to Section 7.3 negative results or supplementary. Optionally run ITAC on actual Gemma 2B text activations (10k tokens) to test whether the null result holds with real activations.
classified_issues.effort_estimate: 1 hour writing reframe + optional 2 GPU hours for real-activation ITAC
classified_issues.verification: ITAC described as negative result in abstract, Introduction, and Conclusion; Section 7.3 contains the detailed analysis

classified_issues.description: Theorem 1 (EDA Lower Bound) proof sketch is informal and circular. The critical claim — that absorption at a partial minimum produces a gradient perturbation of magnitude proportional to delta*||d_c||*sin(theta_{jc}) — is asserted as a given, not derived from the SDL loss stationarity conditions. The formal definition of delta-absorption as a mathematical quantity does not precede the theorem statement (self-reference to 'Section 3' from within Section 3). The quadratic form delta^2 * sin^2(theta_{jc}) / (2 + delta^2) appears without algebraic derivation.
classified_issues.suggestion: Either (a) provide a genuine appendix proof that: (1) formally defines delta-absorption as the perpendicular displacement magnitude at a partial SDL minimum, (2) derives the stationarity conditions of the biconvex SDL loss with respect to encoder weights, (3) shows the perturbation bound step-by-step algebraically; OR (b) downgrade 'Theorem 1' to 'Proposition 1 (Informal Motivation)' with an explicit disclaimer: 'A formal proof requires specifying the biconvex SDL landscape geometry at partial minima, which we leave to future work.' The downgrade is acceptable and does not weaken the paper significantly.
classified_issues.effort_estimate: 1-2 days for formal proof; 1 hour for downgrade
classified_issues.verification: Theorem 1 either has a step-by-step proof in appendix tracing from SDL loss, or is labeled 'Proposition (Informal)' with explicit caveats

classified_issues.description: EDA (1 - cos(encoder_row_j, decoder_col_j)) is nearly identical to the existing SAEBench metric 'encoder_decoder_cosine_sim' (Pearson r > 0.999, reported in the paper itself). The Introduction presents EDA as a new metric without disclosing this pre-existing quantity. The novelty claim depends entirely on the formal theorem (currently informal) and the systematic AUROC evaluation.
classified_issues.suggestion: Add to Section 3 before the metric definition: 'The cosine deviation between encoder row j and decoder column j has been reported in SAEBench (Karvonen et al., 2025) under the name encoder_decoder_cosine_sim. EDA formalizes this observation as a theoretically grounded absorption indicator, contributing (1) a formal lower bound theorem, (2) the first systematic AUROC evaluation against supervised absorption labels, and (3) the D-EDA directional extension. We do not claim EDA as a new metric, but as a principled formalization and empirical validation of an existing signal.' Revise the Introduction to match.
classified_issues.verification: Section 3 Introduction explicitly names SAEBench encoder_decoder_cosine_sim and distinguishes EDA's contribution from it

classified_issues.description: With n_pos=16 out of 65,536 latents at L12-65k (0.024% positive prevalence), AUROC is statistically unreliable regardless of label quality. The pilot go/no-go gate used AUROC at enriched prevalence (pilot n_pos/n_total ~16/100) which does not predict full-dataset AUROC at 0.024% prevalence. The AUROC collapse (0.853 pilot -> 0.468 full) was a predictable consequence of the evaluation design, not just label noise.
classified_issues.suggestion: Add AUPRC (Area Under Precision-Recall Curve) as co-primary metric alongside AUROC for all class-imbalanced settings. The AUPRC values in the data (0.0024 at L12-16k) tell a more honest story. Add to Section 4: 'At positive prevalences below 0.1%, AUROC becomes unstable; we report AUPRC as a more appropriate metric for these settings.' Include AUPRC values in Table 1. Redesign the pilot go/no-go gate for next iteration to use prevalence-matched evaluation.
classified_issues.effort_estimate: 3 hours (AUPRC computation if not cached, writing)
classified_issues.verification: Table 1 includes AUPRC column; Section 4 discussion addresses metric choice under class imbalance

classified_issues.description: The H6 scaling analysis uses canonical target L0 values (L0_source='gemma_scope_paper_canonical'), not measured values. L0 variation across the 6 SAE configurations is 65-72, with no variation within layer for different widths. Partial rho(width | L0) cannot separate width and L0 effects. The 'H6 falsification' claim via partial correlation is methodologically invalid.
classified_issues.suggestion: Replace the partial correlation framing with: 'H6 cannot be properly tested with the current 6 SAE configurations due to insufficient L0 variation within layer-matched width comparisons.' Report only the marginal finding: 'Wider SAEs consistently show higher absorption (marginal Spearman rho=0.35, directional evidence), but the L0-controlled analysis requires configurations with actual L0 variation across widths at the same layer.' Remove 'H6 falsified' language.
classified_issues.verification: Section 7.3 H6 discussion acknowledges the L0 control limitation; no partial correlation claim for H6

classified_issues.description: Three figures (Fig 5, 6, 7) are referenced in the paper text but do not exist as generated PDF files. Generation scripts reference data in phase3e_crossdomain_analysis.json and phase2a_taxonomy.json which exist. Paper cannot be submitted in current state.
classified_issues.suggestion: Execute fig generation scripts immediately: gen_fig5_crossdomain_rates.py (data: phase3e_crossdomain_analysis.json), gen_fig6_ravel_coherence.py, and gen_fig7_subtype_eda.py (data: phase2a_taxonomy.json). Data is available. These are low-effort blockers. Also: (a) resolve SynthSAEBench citation (verify arXiv:2602.14687 or describe as self-constructed), (b) add DeLong & DeLong (1988) citation at first use in Section 4.1.
classified_issues.verification: Figures 5, 6, 7 exist as PDF files in writing/figures/; DeLong citation present in Section 4.1; SynthSAEBench citation resolved

classified_issues.description: The taxonomy's 'early absorption dominance' central finding (72-75%) rests on only 2 SAE configurations (L12-16k underpowered n=16, L12-65k n=65). The paper frames this as 'reframing the absorption problem for the field' without adequate empirical basis. At tau=0.2, the early proportion drops to 32% at L12-65k — the dominance finding is highly threshold-dependent.
classified_issues.suggestion: Expand taxonomy analysis to all 6 Gemma Scope configurations and GPT-2 L6 (which has n=67 absorbed latents with exact labels). Report early/late/partial prevalence across all configurations at multiple thresholds. If dominance holds across configurations, the claim strengthens substantially. If not, downgrade from 'reframes the field' to 'preliminary evidence in two mid-layer configurations.' Add explicit threshold sensitivity table (tau=0.2 to 0.4) to main text.
classified_issues.verification: Taxonomy results for >=4 SAE configurations; early proportion reported at tau=0.2, 0.3, and 0.4 with confidence intervals

success_patterns: Exemplary negative result reporting (Section 7.3): three falsified hypotheses (H5, H6, D-EDA on Gemma) each reported with specific pre-registered target vs. actual result — model scientific writing practice
Statistical rigor: bootstrap CIs (10k resamples), Mann-Whitney U, Kruskal-Wallis, DeLong test comparisons, and Cohen's d effect sizes all appropriately applied
GPT-2 Small as validation anchor: AUROC=0.629 with exact labels on an open model provides the cleanest, most reproducible evidence in the paper — this strategy should be expanded
Result debate mechanism effectively identified over-optimistic claims and generated specific, actionable correction paths
Experiment execution efficiency: actual GPU time ~7 hours vs. planned ~6.5 hours, all 15 tasks completed without failures; GPU utilization on the single allocated GPU was near-continuous
Writing quality (8/10): strong opening line, no filler transitions, no overclaiming phrases, concrete quantitative leads in all sections

issues: EDA ordering claim is factually incorrect. Section 6.2 states 'EDA ordering (late > partial > early) holds at L12-65k (KW p=0.0002)' but phase2a_taxonomy.json shows eda_ordering_holds=false for both L12-16k and L12-65k at all tested thresholds. At L12-65k: partial median EDA (0.652) < early median EDA (0.668) — the full three-way ordering is violated. Table 3 describes early absorption as having 'Low (decoder-absent)' EDA, contradicted by data. This was BLOCKING in iter_0 and remains present in the paper.

EDA (1 - cosine(encoder_j, decoder_j)) is mathematically identical to SAEBench encoder_decoder_cosine_sim (Pearson r > 0.999; R4 delta AUROC = 0.0). Introduction presents EDA as a new metric without disclosing this prior art. The entire novelty claim rests on Theorem 1, which is a proof sketch. A reviewer familiar with SAEBench will flag this immediately.

Theorem 1 (EDA Lower Bound) proof sketch is circular and Appendix B is missing. The critical claim — that absorption at a partial SDL minimum produces a gradient perturbation proportional to delta * ||d_c|| * sin(theta_{jc}) — is asserted, not derived from stationarity conditions. Delta-absorption has no formal mathematical definition before the theorem statement. The bound delta^2*sin^2(theta_{jc})/(2+delta^2) appears without algebraic steps. Appendix B is referenced twice (Sections 3.3 and 7.1) but absent from paper.md.

The three-subtype taxonomy's 'early absorption dominance' finding (72-75%) rests on n=16 (L12-16k, KW p=0.237 — non-significant) and n=65 (L12-65k). The finding is highly threshold-sensitive (tau=0.2: early=32%; tau=0.3: 72%; tau=0.4: 95%). Expansion to all 6 Gemma Scope + 2 GPT-2 configurations was HIGH priority in iter_0 but was not executed. GPT-2 L6 has n_pos=67 exact labels — ideal for taxonomy analysis as a third configuration.

All Gemma Scope AUROC values use Neuronpedia proxy labels. The R4 access check (r4a_gemma_access_check) confirmed Gemma 2 2B remains HuggingFace-gated. This is the single highest-leverage unresolved issue: if 3+/6 Gemma configurations pass AUROC >= 0.65 with exact labels, the score rises from 5.5 to 7.0. The L12-16k pilot result (0.853) that collapsed to 0.468 in the full run at L12-65k suggests the L12-16k proxy label result (0.776) may also be inflated.

D-EDA/EDA equivalence tension is unresolved. R4 direct validation shows EDA = 1 - dec_cos as a mathematical identity (delta AUROC = 0.0 on both GPT-2 configurations). But R3 Phase 5 shows D-EDA AUROC = 0.762 vs EDA AUROC = 0.336 at GPT2-L10 — requiring D-EDA to be a genuinely different calculation. The paper does not reconcile this inconsistency. Table 1's D-EDA column and Section 3.3's LASSO decomposition description are potentially misleading.

AUROC is used as primary metric throughout but is unreliable at extreme class imbalance. At L12-65k: n_pos=16/65536 = 0.024% positive prevalence makes AUROC statistically unstable. AUPRC values exist in source data (phase1_eda_deda_validation.json: AUPRC = 0.0024 at L12-16k) but are not in Table 1 or discussed in main text. This was HIGH priority in iter_0 (issue_07) and was not addressed in iter_1.

Abstract DeLong comparison is ambiguous and factually misleading. The abstract states 'outperforming the decoder cosine baseline by +0.396 AUROC' immediately after 'AUROC = 0.776 on Gemma Scope L12-16k,' making readers infer +0.396 is the L12-16k advantage. The +0.396 is from L5-16k (EDA=0.698, decoder cosine=0.302); the L12-16k difference is +0.553. This is a factual error that will be immediately flagged by reviewers.

Figure 1 (core method diagram: absorption mechanism and EDA geometry) is referenced in Introduction and Section 3.1 ('Figure 1a', 'Figure 1b') but only exists as fig1_absorption_mechanism_desc.md (text descriptor). No PDF was generated. A NeurIPS submission without a method diagram for the paper's core geometric mechanism is a significant gap. The '(see end-of-paper Figures section)' parenthetical is non-standard.

Section 7.1 practical recommendation claims 'focusing on the top 5% of latents by EDA contains approximately 56% of absorbed latents (Prec@50 = 0.0035), reducing supervised evaluation budget by 20x.' These figures are arithmetically inconsistent with source data: top 5% of 16,384 = 819 candidates; 819 × 0.0035 ≈ 2.87 absorbed; 2.87/16 positives ≈ 18%, not 56%. The 20x figure is unexplained. Both figures are unsourced.

ITAC null test on early-type latents does not test what it claims. Table 4's 'NULL' row for early/partial latents reports 0.00% FN reduction — but ITAC was never applied to early-type latents. The 0.00% reflects no correction being attempted, not a selectivity test. The claim 'validates subtype selectivity' is tautological.

H6 scaling analysis uses canonical estimated L0 values (range 65-72, no variation within layer-matched width comparisons). The partial rho(width | L0) cannot separate width from L0 effects when L0 does not vary within the controlled comparison. The 'H6 falsified via partial correlation' language is methodologically invalid. This was flagged in iter_0 issue_08 and remains unchanged.

Three citations missing or unverified: (a) DeLong test first used in Section 4.1 without citation; (b) SynthSAEBench citation 'synthasbench2026' / arXiv:2602.14687 unverified — if internally constructed, the external citation is invalid; (c) Appendix C (RAVEL probe details) is referenced in outline but absent from paper.md. These were in iter_0 issue_09 and remain unresolved.

Section 5.3 intra-RAVEL rho=0.924 inconsistency: this figure comes from the full R3 experiment (n=6 SAE configs, phase3e_crossdomain_analysis.json), while the shuffled control was run on a pilot subset (n=3 configs). Reviewers who notice both sample sizes will question whether the coherence would survive the shuffled control on all 6 configs. The R4 pilot with n=3 gives rho=0.667 (lower than 0.924), suggesting the R3 rho=0.924 may be sensitive to configuration selection.

Polysemanticity stratification AUROC CI is unreliable at n_pos=3. AUROC = 0.922 [0.842, 0.979] on polysemantic latents at L12-16k is presented without noting that n_pos=3 makes bootstrap CIs numerically unreliable. With n_pos < 10, CIs are dominated by sampling artifacts.

'Reframes the absorption problem' overclaims from two configurations. Sections 6.3 and Conclusion use language implying a field-level conclusion based on n=16 (non-significant) and n=65 latents in two SAE configurations with a threshold-sensitive finding. This was flagged in iter_0 lessons and remains unchanged.

GPU parallelization not utilized. All 22 completed tasks ran sequentially on a single GPU (GPU 5). Phase 3 city subtasks (continent/country/language), Phase 5 GPT-2, and R4 subtasks (r4a, r4b, r4c) are mutually independent and could have run in parallel. Estimated wallclock saving: 40-60% if 2+ GPUs available.

Blocking corrections from action plan were not enforced before writing phase. iter_0 action plan listed EDA ordering correction (issue_02) as BLOCKING before further writing, but the writing agent proceeded without applying this correction. The factual error persists in the final paper.

Intra-RAVEL Bonferroni correction: one of three pairwise Spearman comparisons (city-continent vs. city-language: rho=0.886, p=0.019) does NOT survive Bonferroni correction at 0.0071 threshold. Paper uses 'highly coherent' language without noting this. Only 2/3 pairwise comparisons are Bonferroni-significant.
Abstract is 247 words vs. NeurIPS 200-word target. ITAC detail in abstract is 3 sentences for a negative result that should be one clause. Code release sentence belongs in Conclusion, not abstract.

classified_issues.description: EDA ordering claim is factually incorrect. Section 6.2 states 'EDA ordering (late > partial > early) holds at L12-65k (KW p=0.0002)' but phase2a_taxonomy.json shows eda_ordering_holds=false for both L12-16k and L12-65k at all tested thresholds. At L12-65k: partial median EDA (0.652) < early median EDA (0.668) — the full three-way ordering is violated. Table 3 describes early absorption as having 'Low (decoder-absent)' EDA, contradicted by data. This was BLOCKING in iter_0 and remains present in the paper.
classified_issues.suggestion: Replace Section 6.2 claim with: 'The ordering late > early holds at all 5 tested thresholds for both configurations (threshold-stable; L12-65k KW p=0.0002; L12-16k KW p=0.237, underpowered n=16). The predicted full ordering late > partial > early fails: partial-type latents have lower median EDA than early-type latents at both configurations and all thresholds. This suggests decoder-coverage threshold (tau) does not correspond to EDA rank ordering.' Correct Table 3's EDA column for early absorption subtype to reflect actual data (early-type has higher EDA than partial-type). Estimated effort: 1 hour writing. Verification: eda_ordering_holds field in phase2a_taxonomy.json matches the text in Section 6.2.
classified_issues.verification: Section 6.2 text matches eda_ordering_holds=false; Table 3 EDA column for early-type corrected to reflect actual median EDA > partial-type

classified_issues.description: EDA (1 - cosine(encoder_j, decoder_j)) is mathematically identical to SAEBench encoder_decoder_cosine_sim (Pearson r > 0.999; R4 delta AUROC = 0.0). Introduction presents EDA as a new metric without disclosing this prior art. The entire novelty claim rests on Theorem 1, which is a proof sketch. A reviewer familiar with SAEBench will flag this immediately.
classified_issues.suggestion: Add to Section 3.1 before the metric definition: 'The cosine similarity between encoder row j and decoder column j has been reported in SAEBench (Karvonen et al., 2025) under the label encoder_decoder_cosine_sim. EDA's contributions are (1) a formal lower bound theorem connecting encoder-decoder misalignment to absorption degree, (2) the first systematic AUROC evaluation against supervised absorption labels across multiple SAE configurations and model families, and (3) characterization of the operating regime (mid-layer, narrow SAEs). EDA does not introduce a novel formula; its contribution is theoretical grounding and empirical validation of an existing signal.' Revise Introduction accordingly to remove 'we introduce EDA' language. Estimated effort: 1 hour writing. Verification: Introduction and Section 3.1 explicitly name SAEBench encoder_decoder_cosine_sim and distinguish EDA's contributions from it.
classified_issues.verification: Section 3.1 and Introduction explicitly name SAEBench prior metric and reposition EDA as empirical validation

classified_issues.description: Theorem 1 (EDA Lower Bound) proof sketch is circular and Appendix B is missing. The critical claim — that absorption at a partial SDL minimum produces a gradient perturbation proportional to delta * ||d_c|| * sin(theta_{jc}) — is asserted, not derived from stationarity conditions. Delta-absorption has no formal mathematical definition before the theorem statement. The bound delta^2*sin^2(theta_{jc})/(2+delta^2) appears without algebraic steps. Appendix B is referenced twice (Sections 3.3 and 7.1) but absent from paper.md.
classified_issues.suggestion: Two options: (a) Write Appendix B with genuine proof: formally define delta-absorption as the perpendicular displacement magnitude at a partial SDL minimum, state first-order stationarity conditions of the biconvex SDL loss with respect to encoder weights, derive algebraically how child-active inputs create a gradient bias, and show the cosine-distance bound algebraically. (b) Downgrade Theorem 1 to 'Proposition 1 (Intuition)' and add: 'A formal proof requires specifying the biconvex SDL landscape geometry at partial minima, which we leave to future work. The bound is empirically consistent with SynthSAEBench results in Section 3.4.' Write Appendix B with the D-EDA conditioning analysis regardless. Estimated effort: 2 days for formal proof; 1 hour for downgrade + Appendix B minimal content. Verification: Theorem 1 either has step-by-step algebraic derivation in Appendix B, or is labeled Proposition 1 (Intuition) with explicit caveats; Appendix B exists in paper.md.
classified_issues.verification: Theorem 1 has formal proof in appendix or is downgraded to Proposition; Appendix B present in paper.md

classified_issues.description: The three-subtype taxonomy's 'early absorption dominance' finding (72-75%) rests on n=16 (L12-16k, KW p=0.237 — non-significant) and n=65 (L12-65k). The finding is highly threshold-sensitive (tau=0.2: early=32%; tau=0.3: 72%; tau=0.4: 95%). Expansion to all 6 Gemma Scope + 2 GPT-2 configurations was HIGH priority in iter_0 but was not executed. GPT-2 L6 has n_pos=67 exact labels — ideal for taxonomy analysis as a third configuration.
classified_issues.suggestion: Run taxonomy analysis (early/late/partial classification at tau=0.2, 0.25, 0.3, 0.35, 0.4) on: (a) all 6 Gemma Scope configurations using the same Neuronpedia proxy labels as Phase 1; (b) GPT-2 L6 using exact Chanin et al. labels (n_pos=67, already available from Phase 5). Report early/late/partial prevalence across all configurations at multiple thresholds. If dominance holds at 70%+ across 4+ configurations, the finding is defensible. If not, downgrade from 'early dominance' to 'plurality at tau=0.3 in two configurations.' Add a threshold sensitivity table in main text. Estimated effort: 2-3 GPU hours. Verification: Taxonomy results in experiment output JSON for 4+ SAE configurations; early proportion reported at tau=0.2, 0.3, and 0.4 with counts.
classified_issues.verification: Taxonomy analysis completed for 4+ SAE configurations; threshold sensitivity table in main text

classified_issues.description: All Gemma Scope AUROC values use Neuronpedia proxy labels. The R4 access check (r4a_gemma_access_check) confirmed Gemma 2 2B remains HuggingFace-gated. This is the single highest-leverage unresolved issue: if 3+/6 Gemma configurations pass AUROC >= 0.65 with exact labels, the score rises from 5.5 to 7.0. The L12-16k pilot result (0.853) that collapsed to 0.468 in the full run at L12-65k suggests the L12-16k proxy label result (0.776) may also be inflated.
classified_issues.suggestion: Pursue Gemma 2 2B HuggingFace access via the research access application (typically 1-3 days admin). Alternatively, identify another gated model with public SAE releases and labeled absorption data. If neither is feasible within iteration budget, make this the most prominent caveat in the paper: 'All Gemma Scope AUROC values use Neuronpedia proxy labels because Gemma 2 2B remains gated on HuggingFace at time of writing; the only exact-label validation is GPT-2 Small L6 (AUROC=0.629, n_pos=67).' Do not attempt to claim Gemma results are as reliable as GPT-2 results. Estimated effort: 1 day admin + 3-5 GPU hours for re-run. Verification: Phase 1 re-run with exact Chanin et al. labels produces AUROC for all 6 Gemma configurations.
classified_issues.verification: Gemma access obtained and Phase 1 re-run with exact labels, or paper prominently discloses proxy label limitation with quantified uncertainty

classified_issues.description: D-EDA/EDA equivalence tension is unresolved. R4 direct validation shows EDA = 1 - dec_cos as a mathematical identity (delta AUROC = 0.0 on both GPT-2 configurations). But R3 Phase 5 shows D-EDA AUROC = 0.762 vs EDA AUROC = 0.336 at GPT2-L10 — requiring D-EDA to be a genuinely different calculation. The paper does not reconcile this inconsistency. Table 1's D-EDA column and Section 3.3's LASSO decomposition description are potentially misleading.
classified_issues.suggestion: Clarify the D-EDA implementation: (a) If D-EDA is the genuine LASSO-based directional decomposition described in Section 3.3, its AUROC should differ from scalar EDA. Re-implement D-EDA using the LASSO formulation and compute AUROC on GPT-2 configurations; if the result matches R3 (AUROC=0.762 at GPT2-L10), the R4 validation was computing scalar EDA not D-EDA. (b) If D-EDA is merely negated decoder cosine, rename it and remove Section 3.3's LASSO decomposition description. Update Table 1 accordingly. The R4 finding (delta=0.0) suggests (b) is correct, but this must be verified against the actual R3 implementation code. Estimated effort: 2-4 hours code review + recomputation. Verification: R3 D-EDA computation code audited and confirmed to either match or differ from scalar EDA; Table 1 corrected accordingly.
classified_issues.verification: D-EDA implementation clarified; Table 1 and Section 3.3 consistent with actual computation

classified_issues.description: AUROC is used as primary metric throughout but is unreliable at extreme class imbalance. At L12-65k: n_pos=16/65536 = 0.024% positive prevalence makes AUROC statistically unstable. AUPRC values exist in source data (phase1_eda_deda_validation.json: AUPRC = 0.0024 at L12-16k) but are not in Table 1 or discussed in main text. This was HIGH priority in iter_0 (issue_07) and was not addressed in iter_1.
classified_issues.suggestion: Add AUPRC as co-primary metric in Table 1 for all 65k-width configurations (where positive prevalence < 0.05%). Compute AUPRC from cached Phase 1 results (data already available). Add a sentence to Section 4 discussion: 'At positive prevalences below 0.1%, AUROC becomes unstable; we report AUPRC as a complementary metric for 65k-width configurations.' Add a footnote noting that AUPRC = 0.0024 at L12-65k indicates absorption enrichment remains low despite AUROC differences. Estimated effort: 2-3 hours (AUPRC computation if not cached; writing). Verification: Table 1 includes AUPRC column; Section 4 discusses metric choice under class imbalance.
classified_issues.verification: Table 1 includes AUPRC column for 65k-width configs; Section 4 discusses AUROC instability under class imbalance

classified_issues.description: Abstract DeLong comparison is ambiguous and factually misleading. The abstract states 'outperforming the decoder cosine baseline by +0.396 AUROC' immediately after 'AUROC = 0.776 on Gemma Scope L12-16k,' making readers infer +0.396 is the L12-16k advantage. The +0.396 is from L5-16k (EDA=0.698, decoder cosine=0.302); the L12-16k difference is +0.553. This is a factual error that will be immediately flagged by reviewers.
classified_issues.suggestion: Revise abstract to: 'EDA achieves AUROC = 0.776 on Gemma Scope L12-16k [0.700, 0.863], outperforming the decoder cosine baseline by +0.553 AUROC at this configuration (DeLong p ≈ 0), and AUROC = 0.629 on GPT-2 Small L6 [0.561, 0.692] with exact Chanin et al. labels.' This correctly attributes the comparison and introduces the GPT-2 exact-label anchor. Estimated effort: 30 minutes. Verification: Abstract DeLong comparison figure matches the L12-16k result in phase1_eda_deda_validation.json.
classified_issues.verification: Abstract DeLong comparison cites L12-16k figure (+0.553); no ambiguity between L5-16k and L12-16k

classified_issues.description: Figure 1 (core method diagram: absorption mechanism and EDA geometry) is referenced in Introduction and Section 3.1 ('Figure 1a', 'Figure 1b') but only exists as fig1_absorption_mechanism_desc.md (text descriptor). No PDF was generated. A NeurIPS submission without a method diagram for the paper's core geometric mechanism is a significant gap. The '(see end-of-paper Figures section)' parenthetical is non-standard.
classified_issues.suggestion: Generate Figure 1 as a PDF: a geometric diagram illustrating (a) the trained SAE decoder column d_j and encoder row w_{e,j} for a non-absorbed latent (aligned) and an absorbed latent (misaligned due to child absorption), and (b) the EDA geometric interpretation showing the angular deviation theta_{jc}. Use matplotlib or tikz. Place in writing/figures/fig1_absorption_mechanism.pdf. Remove '(see end-of-paper Figures section)' parenthetical and replace with standard 'Figure 1' inline reference. Estimated effort: 2-3 hours. Verification: writing/figures/fig1_absorption_mechanism.pdf exists; Introduction and Section 3.1 references are standard.
classified_issues.verification: writing/figures/fig1_absorption_mechanism.pdf exists; fig references in Introduction and Section 3.1 are standard

classified_issues.description: Section 7.1 practical recommendation claims 'focusing on the top 5% of latents by EDA contains approximately 56% of absorbed latents (Prec@50 = 0.0035), reducing supervised evaluation budget by 20x.' These figures are arithmetically inconsistent with source data: top 5% of 16,384 = 819 candidates; 819 × 0.0035 ≈ 2.87 absorbed; 2.87/16 positives ≈ 18%, not 56%. The 20x figure is unexplained. Both figures are unsourced.
classified_issues.suggestion: Replace with the correctly derived claim: 'At L12-16k, Prec@50 = 0.0035 — absorbed latents are 3.5x enriched in the top-50 EDA candidates versus random selection (baseline positive rate 16/16,384 = 0.098%). Prioritizing the top 5% of the dictionary (819 latents) for supervised inspection reduces the evaluation surface by 20x while maintaining meaningful enrichment of absorbed latents in the inspection set.' Verify the 20x figure is simply 1/0.05 = 20 (inspection budget reduction by dictionary fraction), not a coverage claim. Estimated effort: 30 minutes. Verification: Claim uses correct Prec@50 calculation without unsupported '56%' figure.
classified_issues.verification: Section 7.1 contains correctly-derived Prec@50 claim without '56%' figure

success_patterns: Honest negative result reporting in Section 7.3: three pre-registered hypotheses falsified (H3, H5, H6) with specific targets vs. actual results — reviewer feedback confirms this as the paper's strongest section

R4 adaptive remediation round: GPT-2 exact-label validation and shuffled control were not in original plan but were identified and executed correctly — demonstrates system's ability to self-correct experimental gaps

Statistical methodology: bootstrap CIs (10k resamples), DeLong test, Cohen's d, Mann-Whitney U, Kruskal-Wallis, shuffled null column in Table 1 — appropriate and exemplary; shuffled null column is a model of methodological rigor
GPT-2 as open-model anchor strategy: exact-label validation on accessible models provides the most credible evidence and should anchor every future experimental design
Result debate mechanism effective at filtering overconfidence — retain in all future iterations

issues: Proposition 1's geometric prediction (absorbed pairs have SMALLER decoder-decoder angle) is directly falsified by B1_decoder_geometry.json (AUROC=0.318 for cos² classifier; pos_mean=0.052 < neg_mean=0.127, direction reversed). Paper's three-sentence 'post-convergence equilibrium' reframing in Section 3.3 is asserted without derivation. Introduction and Abstract continue to present P1 as empirically predictive without acknowledging falsification. This is a RECURRING issue: iter_001 lessons_learned flagged 'Theorem 1 proof sketch is circular'; iter_002 supervisor flags same falsification.

Cross-directional metric label count discrepancy: paper states 'proxy labels (n_pos=50)' for AUROC=0.730, but B1_pairwise_eda.json uses threshold=0.29 yielding n_pos=63. Three label sets in play (exact n=18, proxy n=50 in D1/D2, proxy n=63 in B1) are not disambiguated in Table 1. Jaccard overlap 0.115 was computed between n=18 and n=50, not n=63. This is a NEW critical issue surfaced in iter_002 supervisor review.

Practical shortlisting claim 'tractable candidate set for downstream IG-based verification' is directly contradicted by D1_eda_validation.json: Precision@50=0, Precision@100=0, Precision@500=0. Minimum k at which EDA enrichment becomes non-zero is not reported. AUPRC=2.09x base rate (below pre-registration threshold of 3x). This misleading claim appears in Abstract, Section 5.1, and Conclusion. This is a RECURRING issue: iter_001 noted EDA AUPRC insufficient; iter_002 supervisor flags same Precision@k contradiction.

EDA_norm (AUROC=0.738) significantly outperforms EDA_base (AUROC=0.650, DeLong p=0.0007) — statistically significant improvement. Paper leads all summary claims with EDA_base, burying EDA_norm as a variant. EDA_norm = EDA_base × ||enc_j|| and its superiority is mechanistically consistent with P2 (absorbed features develop elevated encoder norms under competing gradient pressure). This suppression of a stronger metric introduces systematic downward bias in reported performance. NEW in iter_002.

Four critical numerical inconsistencies spanning multiple paper locations: (a) Phantom AUROC=0.206 in Introduction and Conclusion (Table 1 shows 0.350 for raw cosine); (b) Absorption rate range: Introduction '92-97%' vs Section 4.4 '0.876-0.978' vs Conclusion '0.919-0.968' — three-way inconsistency; (c) Spearman rho: Introduction ρ=0.191 vs Section 4.4 ρ=-0.482 (all configs) vs ρ=-0.100 (jb only) — different sign and magnitude; (d) Table 1 caption says n_pos=50 for cross-directional while raw data shows n_pos=63. ALL four items are RECURRING from iter_001.

Missing Abstract: paper_draft.md begins directly with Introduction. Outline contains a full draft abstract. A paper without an abstract cannot be submitted. Section numbering is inconsistent: Introduction/Related Work unnumbered, Method uses 3.x, Experiments uses '# 4 Experiments' explicit numbering. Roadmap sentence at end of Introduction describes reversed paper flow (says Related Work comes after Method). Figure 1 is referenced twice (line 82 and line 404). All four are RECURRING structural issues from iter_001.

ASI (originally proposed probe-free detector) was falsified (AUROC=0.4215, below null mean=0.4973) and is not the paper's primary contribution, yet the paper does not clearly state this discovery path. Reviewers familiar with the original proposal structure may be confused about why ASI fails while EDA succeeds. This reduces scientific transparency and risks reviewer credibility challenges. This is a RECURRING issue: iter_001 flagged EDA=SAEBench metric disclosure; iter_002 flags ASI failure narrative gap.

B3 cross-architecture comparison (Standard L1 vs TopK) uses different hook points: Standard uses resid_pre at layer 6, TopK uses resid_post at layer 6. This confound is documented in audit_report.json but not acknowledged in the paper. The paper presents TopK's 'weaker EDA signal' as evidence for training regime effects, but hook-point difference is a competing explanation. NEW in iter_002.

Reproducibility gaps: sae-spelling repository commit hash, FeatureAbsorptionCalculator parameters (threshold, n_tokens, letter set), and SAELens release hash are absent from Section 3.4. The 18 absorbed feature IDs are in D1_eda_validation.json (label_source_file: iter_001/exp/results/r4/r4a_direct_labels.json) but not in the paper. Replication with different sae-spelling version could yield different absorbed feature IDs and a different AUROC. RECURRING from iter_001.

Encoder norm (AUROC=0.757, AUPRC=5.68x) outperforms EDA_base (AUROC=0.650) and the difference is NOT statistically significant (DeLong p=0.153). Paper treats encoder norm as 'confounded baseline' but does not provide evidence distinguishing absorption-driven norm inflation from polysemanticity-driven norm inflation. The paper cannot claim EDA is more mechanistically interpretable without supporting evidence. NEW in iter_002 (not in iter_001 lessons).

Cross-domain semantic hierarchy experiments (animate_inanimate, noun_proper) show absorption_rate=9.99e-9 (zero) with n=20 words each. Result is critically underpowered: to detect 5% absorption rate above null with 80% power requires n≈500 events. The null result does not support 'semantic absorption is absent in GPT-2 Small' — only that it was not detected with methods and sample sizes used. Furthermore, the animate_inanimate probe achieves F1=1.0 on n_test=22 without shuffle control reported (city_country was excluded after failing shuffle at F1=0.621). RECURRING from iter_001.

EDA magnitude tension (mean EDA=0.671 ≈ 48° angle vs. small theta predicted at onset) appears in both Section 4.2 and Section 5.2 in nearly identical language, signaling unresolved handling. Paper presented same tension twice without deciding how to present it. RECURRING from iter_001.

Hysteresis experiment (Section 4.4) is uninterpretable due to ceiling saturation. Fine-tuned SAE: absorption 0.959 → 0.960 (unchanged). From-scratch: 0.964. Three equally consistent hypotheses (hysteresis, universal absorption in this L0 range, measurement noise) are all consistent with the data. Conclusion claims 'consistent with the absorbed state being metastable' — this is incomplete and not well-supported. RECURRING from iter_001.

Scope limitation: all positive EDA results are GPT-2 Small (117M) only. No Gemma Scope experiments despite being in original proposal. Most NeurIPS reviewers will consider GPT-2 Small-only as minimal proof-of-concept. AJT polarity reversal (all 3 SAEs show EDA_delta < 0) limits the claim to L1-penalized SAEs at mid-layers. Scope acknowledged but limits practical utility claims. RECURRING from iter_001 (Gemma access blocked).

PIPELINE BLOCKING ISSUE: iter_001 action plan flagged EDA ordering facts error and phantom AUROC numbers as BLOCKING before any further writing. Writing agent in iter_002 proceeded WITHOUT verifying these blocking conditions. The pipeline has no machine-verifiable gate to enforce BLOCKING issues before writing starts. This creates a systemic loop where the same issues recur every iteration. This is a RECURRING pipeline issue.

classified_issues.description: Proposition 1's geometric prediction (absorbed pairs have SMALLER decoder-decoder angle) is directly falsified by B1_decoder_geometry.json (AUROC=0.318 for cos² classifier; pos_mean=0.052 < neg_mean=0.127, direction reversed). Paper's three-sentence 'post-convergence equilibrium' reframing in Section 3.3 is asserted without derivation. Introduction and Abstract continue to present P1 as empirically predictive without acknowledging falsification. This is a RECURRING issue: iter_001 lessons_learned flagged 'Theorem 1 proof sketch is circular'; iter_002 supervisor flags same falsification.
classified_issues.suggestion: Reorder three contributions: (1) EDA empirical detection first, (2) RD framework as theoretical motivation for when absorption is loss-preferred (NOT a geometric predictor), (3) Proposition 2 as mechanistic conjecture bridging theory and EDA. Add one sentence to Section 3.2: 'Onset geometry (small theta_{p,c}) does not characterize post-convergence state; B1 shows absorbed features have LARGER decoder angle post-convergence (AUROC=0.318 < 0.5).' Cost: 2-3 sentences + contribution reordering. Verify: B1_decoder_geometry.json AUROC=0.318 cited in paper.

classified_issues.description: Cross-directional metric label count discrepancy: paper states 'proxy labels (n_pos=50)' for AUROC=0.730, but B1_pairwise_eda.json uses threshold=0.29 yielding n_pos=63. Three label sets in play (exact n=18, proxy n=50 in D1/D2, proxy n=63 in B1) are not disambiguated in Table 1. Jaccard overlap 0.115 was computed between n=18 and n=50, not n=63. This is a NEW critical issue surfaced in iter_002 supervisor review.
classified_issues.suggestion: Audit chain from B1_pairwise_eda.json (threshold=0.29, n_pos=63) to paper's stated n_pos=50. Add dedicated column header in Table 1 clearly showing label set per row: 'exact n=18 / proxy n=50 / proxy n=63'. Recompute Jaccard overlap if proxy set is n=63. This is a single Python audit + table update. Cost: ~30 min. Verify: Table 1 footnotes explicitly identify which rows use which label set.

classified_issues.description: Practical shortlisting claim 'tractable candidate set for downstream IG-based verification' is directly contradicted by D1_eda_validation.json: Precision@50=0, Precision@100=0, Precision@500=0. Minimum k at which EDA enrichment becomes non-zero is not reported. AUPRC=2.09x base rate (below pre-registration threshold of 3x). This misleading claim appears in Abstract, Section 5.1, and Conclusion. This is a RECURRING issue: iter_001 noted EDA AUPRC insufficient; iter_002 supervisor flags same Precision@k contradiction.
classified_issues.suggestion: Replace 'tractable candidate set' with 'statistical enrichment at distributional level (AUROC=0.650, AUPRC=2.09x base rate)'. Report minimum k at which Precision@k > 0 (compute from D1 rank order). Reframe Abstract claim: remove 'first probe-free statistical shortlist method'. Correct framing: 'EDA provides ~9x enrichment vs. exhaustive sweep in the top-1000 candidate pool'. Cost: prose changes only + one Python computation. Verify: Abstract and Conclusion no longer use 'shortlist' or 'tractable candidate set'.

classified_issues.description: EDA_norm (AUROC=0.738) significantly outperforms EDA_base (AUROC=0.650, DeLong p=0.0007) — statistically significant improvement. Paper leads all summary claims with EDA_base, burying EDA_norm as a variant. EDA_norm = EDA_base × ||enc_j|| and its superiority is mechanistically consistent with P2 (absorbed features develop elevated encoder norms under competing gradient pressure). This suppression of a stronger metric introduces systematic downward bias in reported performance. NEW in iter_002.
classified_issues.suggestion: Promote EDA_norm (AUROC=0.738) as primary reported EDA variant in Table 1, Abstract, Introduction, and Conclusion. Report EDA_base (AUROC=0.650) as secondary diagnostic. Add one sentence explaining the mechanistic connection: EDA_norm weights by encoder magnitude, consistent with P2's competing-gradient interpretation. Cost: Table update + prose changes. Verify: Abstract uses AUROC=0.738, not 0.650.

classified_issues.description: Four critical numerical inconsistencies spanning multiple paper locations: (a) Phantom AUROC=0.206 in Introduction and Conclusion (Table 1 shows 0.350 for raw cosine); (b) Absorption rate range: Introduction '92-97%' vs Section 4.4 '0.876-0.978' vs Conclusion '0.919-0.968' — three-way inconsistency; (c) Spearman rho: Introduction ρ=0.191 vs Section 4.4 ρ=-0.482 (all configs) vs ρ=-0.100 (jb only) — different sign and magnitude; (d) Table 1 caption says n_pos=50 for cross-directional while raw data shows n_pos=63. ALL four items are RECURRING from iter_001.
classified_issues.suggestion: (a) Replace AUROC=0.206 with 0.350 everywhere (3 locations). (b) Use '0.876-0.978' uniformly — verify from E1_phase_transition.json; add jb-suite parenthetical '(0.938-0.967 for primary jb suite)' in Section 4.4 only. (c) Choose canonical Spearman rho=−0.482 (E1, absorption_rate vs 1/L0, all 11 configs) for phase stability claim; use consistently in Introduction and Section 4.4; label ρ=0.191 explicitly as EDA_delta vs 1/L0 if retained. (d) See W002. Total cost: find-and-replace + prose. Verify: paper.md contains exactly one value for each statistic with consistent sourcing.

classified_issues.description: Missing Abstract: paper_draft.md begins directly with Introduction. Outline contains a full draft abstract. A paper without an abstract cannot be submitted. Section numbering is inconsistent: Introduction/Related Work unnumbered, Method uses 3.x, Experiments uses '# 4 Experiments' explicit numbering. Roadmap sentence at end of Introduction describes reversed paper flow (says Related Work comes after Method). Figure 1 is referenced twice (line 82 and line 404). All four are RECURRING structural issues from iter_001.
classified_issues.suggestion: Add abstract from writing/outline.md before Introduction (check for the four numerical inconsistencies in W005 before adding). Standardize section numbering to explicit LaTeX-style (1 Introduction, 2 Related Work, 3 Method, 4 Experiments, 5 Discussion, 6 Conclusion). Fix roadmap sentence to correctly describe linear section order. Remove orphan Figure 1 reference at line 82. Cost: ~1 hour editing. Verify: paper.md compiles without duplicate figures, section numbers match sequentially.

classified_issues.description: ASI (originally proposed probe-free detector) was falsified (AUROC=0.4215, below null mean=0.4973) and is not the paper's primary contribution, yet the paper does not clearly state this discovery path. Reviewers familiar with the original proposal structure may be confused about why ASI fails while EDA succeeds. This reduces scientific transparency and risks reviewer credibility challenges. This is a RECURRING issue: iter_001 flagged EDA=SAEBench metric disclosure; iter_002 flags ASI failure narrative gap.
classified_issues.suggestion: Add one sentence to Section 3.3 or Section 4.1: 'The originally proposed Absorption Susceptibility Index (ASI = cos²(θ_{p,c}) × freq_ratio) failed empirical validation (AUROC=0.4215, below null mean); EDA was identified as the operative geometric signal during exploration.' This is intellectually honest and aligns with the paper's exemplary negative result reporting. Cost: 1 sentence. Verify: Section contains explicit ASI failure statement.

classified_issues.description: B3 cross-architecture comparison (Standard L1 vs TopK) uses different hook points: Standard uses resid_pre at layer 6, TopK uses resid_post at layer 6. This confound is documented in audit_report.json but not acknowledged in the paper. The paper presents TopK's 'weaker EDA signal' as evidence for training regime effects, but hook-point difference is a competing explanation. NEW in iter_002.
classified_issues.suggestion: Add explicit caveat sentence to Section 4.3: 'Note that TopK SAE uses blocks.6.hook_resid_post while Standard SAE uses blocks.6.hook_resid_pre; this hook-point difference is a confound that may contribute to the EDA signal difference independently of sparsity formulation.' Cost: 1 sentence. Verify: Section 4.3 contains confound caveat; no causal claim about sparsity formulation without qualification.

classified_issues.description: Reproducibility gaps: sae-spelling repository commit hash, FeatureAbsorptionCalculator parameters (threshold, n_tokens, letter set), and SAELens release hash are absent from Section 3.4. The 18 absorbed feature IDs are in D1_eda_validation.json (label_source_file: iter_001/exp/results/r4/r4a_direct_labels.json) but not in the paper. Replication with different sae-spelling version could yield different absorbed feature IDs and a different AUROC. RECURRING from iter_001.
classified_issues.suggestion: Add to Section 3.4: (1) sae-spelling repository URL and commit hash; (2) FeatureAbsorptionCalculator parameters (threshold=0.32 from D2 config, n_tokens=3126, letters evaluated); (3) SAELens release identifier 'gpt2-small-res-jb'; (4) the 18 absorbed feature IDs as an appendix table. Cost: ~30 min fact-gathering from D1/D2 JSON files. Verify: Section 3.4 contains all four items.

classified_issues.description: Encoder norm (AUROC=0.757, AUPRC=5.68x) outperforms EDA_base (AUROC=0.650) and the difference is NOT statistically significant (DeLong p=0.153). Paper treats encoder norm as 'confounded baseline' but does not provide evidence distinguishing absorption-driven norm inflation from polysemanticity-driven norm inflation. The paper cannot claim EDA is more mechanistically interpretable without supporting evidence. NEW in iter_002 (not in iter_001 lessons).
classified_issues.suggestion: Either: (a) accept encoder norm as primary empirical detector and reframe EDA as providing mechanistic directional insight; or (b) provide evidence that EDA separates from encoder norm in polysemanticity-controlled subset (check Neuronpedia or logit-based polysemanticity estimates for the 18 exact positives vs negatives). Note: if EDA_norm is promoted per W004, DeLong comparison should shift to EDA_norm vs encoder_norm. Cost: 1-2 sentences clarification or ~2 hr experiment. Verify: paper does not claim EDA superiority over encoder norm without statistical support.

success_patterns: Negative result reporting: three falsified hypotheses (H1 direction, H3 ASI, H4 phase transition) each reported with exact pre-registered target vs. actual result — model scientific writing practice.
Statistical rigor: bootstrap CIs (10k resamples), permutation null z-scores, DeLong test comparisons, Cohen's d effect sizes all appropriately applied.
GPT-2 Small as validation anchor: open model with exact Chanin labels provides cleanest, most reproducible evidence.
Audit system (79 claims, 0 discrepancies in report): rigorous claim-evidence linkage demonstrates scientific integrity.
Cross-directional metric cos(ê_p, d_c) AUROC=0.730 with z=6.38 is the paper's strongest empirical result and is correctly presented as empirical discovery.
